articles.title: Amit Shah to Review National Flood and Heatwave Preparedness - The Chenab Times
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Delhi
articles.full_text:
New Delhi: Union Home Minister Amit Shah is scheduled to chair a critical high-level meeting in the national capital on May 10, 2026, to conduct a comprehensive review of India’s preparedness for potential flood and heatwave conditions across the country. The meeting underscores the government’s proactive approach to disaster management, emphasizing a ‘Whole of Government and Whole of Society’ strategy under Prime Minister Narendra Modi.
Information was available with The Chenab Times that the Ministry of Home Affairs, under the guidance of Union Home Minister Amit Shah, has significantly bolstered the nation’s disaster management framework in recent years. This includes enhancing coordination between central and state government agencies, strengthening the National Disaster Response Force (NDRF), and maintaining a consistent focus on a ‘zero casualty’ approach during natural calamities.
The impending review meeting signifies the current administration’s steadfast commitment to proactive governance, citizen-centric disaster management strategies, and the overarching goal of building a disaster-resilient India. The Union Home Minister will assess the readiness of central government agencies, scrutinize early warning systems, and evaluate resource deployment and inter-agency coordination mechanisms. The objective is to ensure minimal loss of life and property during potential natural disasters.
Furthermore, Union Home Minister Amit Shah is expected to review the progress of long-term flood mitigation measures being implemented nationwide. This includes the protection of critical infrastructure projects, river management initiatives, and the integration of scientific advancements in forecasting capabilities. The meeting will also involve a review of the implementation status of decisions taken during the previous year’s review meeting, ensuring accountability and facilitating the accelerated, coordinated execution of disaster preparedness activities.
Key areas slated for discussion during the high-level meeting include the strengthening of flood forecasting and early warning systems through the adoption of advanced technology and real-time data integration. Emphasis will also be placed on enhancing preparedness to manage simultaneous disasters, particularly addressing the challenges posed by the convergence of floods and heatwaves. Community awareness and resilience-building programs aimed at preventing loss of life and property will be a significant focus. Additionally, the minister will review the adequacy of essential relief materials and the state of medical preparedness across the country.

articles.title: Delhi experiences rain and cooler temperatures after heatwave - The News Mill
articles.full_text:
After nearly a week of intense heat with temperatures nearing 44°C, residents of New Delhi experienced relief on the morning of April 29, 2026, as rain and gusty winds brought cooler conditions.
Several areas across the national capital were lashed by rain early in the day, accompanied by low-hanging clouds that covered the sky. The showers contributed to a reduction in temperatures and alleviated the persistent humidity.
A sudden change in weather patterns brought dark clouds, strong winds, and refreshing rain showers to parts of the city including Kushak Road and the Lutyens’ Delhi area. The city had been under a “Yellow Alert” for heatwaves, with temperatures peaking between 42°C and 45°C in recent days.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) had forecast that a western disturbance would trigger atmospheric activity, which rapidly lowered the temperature. The maximum temperature, initially predicted to hit 41°C, was expected to fall to around 38°C following the rainfall.
The Regional Weather Forecast Centre (RWFC) in New Delhi indicated that light to moderate rain, accompanied by thunderstorms, lightning, and gusty winds of 30-40 km/h, was very likely across Delhi and surrounding regions during the morning hours. RWFC specified that such weather was expected over the entire Delhi and National Capital Region, as well as in nearby areas of Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, and Rajasthan.
Additionally, RWFC reported that light rainfall was very likely in several other locations including Rajaund, Assandh, Safidon, Barwala, Jind, Panipat, Hissar, Gohana, Gannaur, Hansi, Siwani, Meham, Tosham, Bhiwani, Loharu (Haryana), and Shamli.
The onset of strong winds and intermittent rain showers brought a marked change in Delhi’s weather, offering respite from recent heatwave-like conditions. Kushak Road exhibited cloudy skies and wet roads following the rainfall.
Meanwhile, data from the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) showed Delhi’s air quality index at 260, categorised as “poor”.
On April 28, 2026, IMD scientist Akhil Srivastava noted a weather alert covering northern, eastern, and western India. He explained that thunderstorm activity was anticipated in Punjab and Haryana over the coming days, potentially causing temperatures to drop by 2-4 degrees Celsius.
Srivastava added that an orange alert for thunderstorms had been issued for Bihar, Gangetic West Bengal, Odisha, and Jharkhand, with heavy to very heavy rainfall forecast in northeastern India during the following week.
He said: “In the northern region, a heatwave has been prevailing for the past 15 days. However, due to a western disturbance centred around 66° East longitude, thunderstorm activity is expected in the Western Himalayas and adjoining plains. Alerts have been issued for Jammu and Kashmir, Himachal Pradesh and Uttarakhand for thunderstorms and hailstorms. Punjab and Haryana may witness thunderstorms with wind speeds of up to 60 km/h, leading to a drop in maximum temperatures by 2-4°C over the next three days.”
Regarding heatwave conditions and rainfall prospects, he stated: “Heatwave conditions are likely in parts of Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Vidarbha and Marathwada, but are expected to ease thereafter. Eastern India, including Bihar, West Bengal, Jharkhand and Odisha, is under an orange alert for severe thunderstorms, with wind speeds reaching up to 60 km/h and possible hail. Northeastern India is forecast to receive heavy to extremely heavy rainfall over the next week, with red alerts issued for Assam, Meghalaya, Nagaland, Manipur, Mizoram and Tripura.”
